text:
The International Olympic Committee has defended its boxing eligibility criteria after facing backlash for allowing two athletes to enter the women’s competition at the Paris Olympics after failing gender tests in the past.

The International Olympic Committee has defended its decision to permit two athletes to compete in the women’s boxing competition in Paris after being disqualified from previous championships for failing gender verification tests.

Lin Yu-ting from Taiwan, who is a two-time world champion, and Imane Khelif from Algeria are both competing in the 2024 Paris Olympics.

Both had previously competed at the Tokyo Olympics in 2021, but were disqualified from last year’s World Championships after failing the gender verification tests, reportedly having “XY chromosomes”, according to The Times.

Spokesman for the International Olympic Committee Mark Adams on Tuesday said both women had qualified to compete in the women's competition under the Olympics eligibility criteria.

“Everyone competing in the women’s category is complying with the competition eligibility rules,” Mr Adams said.

“They are women in their passports and it’s stated that this is the case, that they are female.

“These athletes have competed many times before for many years. They haven’t just suddenly arrived.”

Khelif will compete in the under 66kg ­cate­gory on Thursday, and Lin will face off in the under 57kg cate­gory.

Women’s sports activist and former competitive swimmer for the University of Kentucky Riley Gaines lashed out at the Olympic Committee, accusing it of glorifying “men punching women in the face with the intent of knocking them unconscious”.

“A woman is going to die,” she posted on X.

“Imagine training your WHOLE life, getting good enough to earn a spot on the Olympic team, hoping to win an Olympic medal....then you're told you have to fight a man.

“That's the tragic reality for these women.”

Football commentator and women’s sport advocate Lucy Zelic  also slammed the International Olympic Committee for allowing two Zambian women’s football players to compete despite also previously failing gender eligibility tests.

Zambia’s star forward Barbra Banda and striker Racheal Kundananji were both banned from playing in the Africa Cup of Nations tournament for having naturally high testosterone levels.

“We are entitled to ask questions. We are entitled to know why the failed gender verification tests conducted in 2022 were completely ignored by FIFA in 2023 and continue to be ignored in 2024,” Zelic wrote on X.

“That the International Olympics Committee do not have ‘as strict’ rules when it comes to gender testing is also a slight on the credibility of the organisation and the sports being contested.”
